tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line systemic therapy for HLA-A*02:01-positive disease

trial_data.trial_name: Phase III randomized trial (Nathan P, et al. N Engl J Med 2021;385:1196-1206)
trial_data.notes: Tebentafusp is a bispecific gp100-HLA-A*02:01-directed T-cell receptor and CD3 T-cell engager. Phase III trial compared tebentafusp to investigator's choice (pembrolizumab, ipilimumab, or dacarbazine). 1-year OS 73% vs 59%. Median OS in tebentafusp arm was 21.6 months at 3-year analysis (Hassel JC, et al. N Engl J Med 2023;389:2256-2266). Response rates are low, so symptomatic patients may be better palliated by liver-directed treatment first or ipilimumab/nivolumab [UM-6A, UMSYS-1 1 of 2].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Recommended as first-line therapy for HLA-A*02:01-positive metastatic uveal melanoma. Agents effective for metastatic cutaneous melanoma may be used after progression on tebentafusp for HLA-A*02:01-positive disease and as first-line for HLA-A*02:01-negative disease. Hospitalization recommended for first cycles due to cytokine release syndrome risk [UMSYS-1 1 of 2].

drugs.name: nivolumab
drugs.schedule: every 3 weeks for 4 cycles (induction), then 3 mg/kg every 2 weeks (maintenance)

trial_data.trial_name: Single-arm phase II study (Pelster MS, et al. J Clin Oncol 2021;39:599-607)
trial_data.notes: Grade 3/4 treatment-related adverse events in 40% of patients. Another phase II trial (GEM-1402, Piulats JM, et al. J Clin Oncol 2021;39:586-598) in treatment-naïve patients: 12-month OS 51.9%, median OS 12.7 months, median PFS 3.0 months. Doses used: nivolumab 1 mg/kg + ipilimumab 3 mg/kg; other dosing regimens (e.g., nivolumab 3 mg/kg + ipilimumab 1 mg/kg) unknown effectiveness in uveal melanoma [UMSYS-1 1 of 2].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Immune checkpoint inhibitors have inferior activity in uveal melanoma compared to cutaneous melanoma, but may produce durable benefit in a minority. Response rates 5%–20% for nivolumab/ipilimumab. NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities should be followed. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab but is not approved for concurrent use with IV ipilimumab [UMSYS-1 1 of 2].

drugs.name: ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: every 3 weeks for 4 cycles (induction), then every 12 weeks (maintenance)

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II DeCOG study (Zimmer L, et al. PLoS One 2015;10:e0118564)
trial_data.notes: Disease control rate at 12 weeks was 47%; at 24 weeks 21%. Grade 3-4 adverse events in 36%. Another phase II study (Piulats et al, GEM-1 trial) reported median OS 9.8 months, partial response 6.5%, grade 3-4 AEs in 10% [UMSYS-2 1 of 2].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Response rates for ipilimumab monotherapy in uveal melanoma are 0%–5%. Limited clinical activity. Also listed as other recommended. Dosing based on published trials; footnote in guideline mentions small prospective studies showing 0%–5% response rates for anti-PD-1 or ipilimumab monotherapy [UMSYS-1 1 of 2].

trial_data.trial_name: Retrospective and prospective studies (Kottschade LA, et al. Melanoma Res 2016;26:300-303; Algazi AP, et al. Cancer 2016;122:3344-3353)
trial_data.notes: Response rates 0%–5% for anti-PD-1 monotherapy. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab with different dosing instructions.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is an anti-PD-1 antibody. NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities should be followed. Recommended as other recommended regimen for metastatic uveal melanoma [UMSYS-1 1 of 2].

trial_data.trial_name: Prospective studies (van der Kooij MK, et al. Acta Oncol 2017;56:101-103; Johnson DB, et al. J Natl Compr Canc Netw 2019;17:114-117; Rossi E, et al. Cancer Immunol Immunother 2019;68:1179-1185)
trial_data.notes: Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab with different dosing and administration instructions.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab is an anti-PD-1 antibody. Response rates 0%–5%. NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities should be followed. Recommended as other recommended regimen [UMSYS-1 1 of 2].

trial_data.trial_name: Review (Serrone L, et al. J Exp Clin Cancer Res 2000;19:21-34)
trial_data.notes: Part of investigator's choice control arm in tebentafusp phase III trial. Used as comparator in several studies.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cytotoxic regimen considered useful in certain circumstances for metastatic uveal melanoma. Order of listed therapies does not reflect preference. Choice based on individual patient evaluation [UMSYS-1 1 of 2].

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II evaluation (Bedikian AY, et al. Melanoma Res 2003;13:303-306)
trial_data.notes: Also used as comparator in phase II trials for metastatic uveal melanoma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cytotoxic regimen. Useful in certain circumstances. No specific dose or schedule provided in guideline [UMSYS-1 1 of 2].

trial_data.trial_name: Review (Wiernik PH, Einzig AI. J Natl Cancer Inst Monogr 1993;15:185-187)
trial_data.notes: Single-agent activity in melanoma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cytotoxic regimen for metastatic uveal melanoma. Useful in certain circumstances [UMSYS-1 1 of 2].

trial_data.trial_name: Phase 2 clinical trial (Hersh EM, et al. Cancer 2010;116:155-163)
trial_data.notes: Also studied in combination with carboplatin (Kottschade LA, et al. Cancer 2011;117:1704-1710).

tier_1_regimens.notes: Also referred to as nab-paclitaxel. Useful in certain circumstances for metastatic uveal melanoma [UMSYS-1 1 of 2].

trial_data.trial_name: Phase III trial (Flaherty KT, et al. J Clin Oncol 2013;31:373-379)
trial_data.notes: Double-blind randomized phase III trial of carboplatin/paclitaxel ± sorafenib. No significant differences in OS, PFS, or response rate. Sorafenib arm had more toxicity. Also evaluated in second-line (Rao RD, et al. Cancer 2006;106:375-382) and in uveal-specific studies (Homsi J, et al. Melanoma Res 2010;20:507-510).

tier_1_regimens.notes: Combination chemotherapy regimen. Useful in certain circumstances for metastatic uveal melanoma. Doses not specified in guideline [UMSYS-1 1 of 2].

trial_data.trial_name: Phase 1 dose-escalation trial (Falchook GS, et al. Lancet Oncol 2012;13:782-789); Randomized phase 2 study (Shoushtari AN, et al. J Clin Oncol 2016;34:9511)
trial_data.notes: MEK inhibitor. Selumetinib (another MEK inhibitor) showed modest PFS benefit in phase II (median 3.7 vs 1.6 months) but no OS benefit. Trametinib is listed due to similarity. BRAF and KIT mutations are rare in uveal melanoma; the guideline notes these options do not cover BRAF- or KIT-mutated tumors.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Targeted therapy useful in certain circumstances. Uveal melanomas rarely have BRAF or KIT mutations, but often have GNAQ/GNA11 mutations activating MEK pathway. Manage toxicities per NCCN Guidelines for Melanoma: Cutaneous [UMSYS-1 1 of 2].

tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Consider as one or more liver-directed therapies

trial_data.trial_name: Phase III SCANDIUM trial (Olofsson Bagge R, et al. J Clin Oncol 2023;41:3042-3050); Phase II trial (Hughes MS, et al. Ann Surg Oncol 2016;23:1309-1319)
trial_data.notes: Used in patients with hepatic-dominant metastatic uveal melanoma with <50% liver involvement. Overnight stay in ICU recommended for hemodynamic monitoring. Recommended only at experienced centers. Prescribing information available at FDA label.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Regional isolation perfusion technique. Percutaneous hepatic perfusion with melphalan. Response rate improved over best alternative care but OS not significantly different. Part of liver-directed therapy options for hepatic-dominant disease [UM-6A].

tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Recommended option for tumors ≤19 mm basal diameter and ≤10 mm thickness

trial_data.trial_name: COMS randomized trial (COMS Report No. 28. Arch Ophthalmol 2006;124:1684-1693)
trial_data.notes: No difference in cause-specific survival compared to enucleation for medium tumors. 5-year and 10-year disease-specific death: brachytherapy 10% and 18%; enucleation 11% and 17%.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Plaque brachytherapy is a common definitive radiotherapy for primary tumor. Iodine-125 COMS plaques: 85 Gy prescribed to tumor apex at low dose-rate (≥0.6 Gy/h). Plaque margin ≥2 mm on tumor border. Largest commercially available plaque is 23 mm diameter, thus recommended for tumors with largest basal diameter ≤19 mm. Performed by experienced multidisciplinary team [UM-B 1 of 4].

trial_data.notes: No specific trial data provided for each isotope; dosing based on institutional experience.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Other radioisotopes include ruthenium-106, palladium-103, strontium-90, cobalt-60, cesium-131, or non-COMS iodine-125 plaques. Dose: 60–100 Gy prescribed at low dose-rate to tumor apex; alternatively, a minimum dose prescribed to tumor base. Plaque margin may vary for other isotopes [UM-B 1 of 4].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Definitive primary treatment or for intraocular/orbital recurrence

trial_data.trial_name: Prospective randomized trial (Mishra KK, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2015;92:376-383); UCSF-LBNL randomized trial
trial_data.notes: No difference in cause-specific survival between plaque brachytherapy and particle beam for tumors ≤15 mm basal diameter and ≤11 mm apical height.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Protons: 50–70 cobalt Gray equivalent (CGyE) in 4–5 fractions prescribed to planning target volume. Fiducial markers (tantalum clips) encouraged. Volumetric planning in 3 dimensions encouraged. Performed by experienced multidisciplinary team. Also appropriate after margin-positive enucleation or for recurrence [UM-B 2 of 4].

trial_data.trial_name: Phase I/II dose-escalation study (Tsuji H, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2007;67:857-862)
trial_data.notes: Dose escalation study for locally advanced or unfavorably located choroidal melanoma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Carbon ions: 60–85 CGyE in 5 fractions prescribed to planning target volume. Fiducial markers encouraged. Volumetric planning encouraged [UM-B 2 of 4].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Definitive treatment (least often used for primary tumors)
tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Option for primary or recurrent intraocular tumors

trial_data.trial_name: Prospective studies (Muller K, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2005;63:116-122; Zehetmayer M, et al. Radiother Oncol 2000;55:135-144)
trial_data.notes: Few prospective studies assessed efficacy and safety.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Fractionated SRS: 45–70 Gy in 2–5 fractions. Tumor localization, fiducial marker use, and planning generally consistent with particle beam therapy approaches [UM-B 2 of 4].
Single-fraction SRS: 18–45 Gy in 1 fraction. Least often used form of definitive radiotherapy for primary or recurrent intraocular tumors [UM-B 2 of 4].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Fractionated Photon Radiotherapy (Adjuvant for Orbital Involvement)
tier_1_regimens.site: Uveal melanoma with orbital involvement after surgery
tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant to surgery (margin-positive enucleation or exenteration, or resected regional metastases)
tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Preferred option as adjuvant to surgery for orbital involvement

trial_data.notes: Dosing based on retrospective studies and COMS (COMS report no. 11; Ang KK, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1994;30:795-798).

tier_1_regimens.notes: Adjuvant radiotherapy for patients at risk for local recurrence (margin-positive enucleation or exenteration) or regional recurrence (resected regional metastases). Dose: 20–30 Gy in 5 fractions prescribed to clinical target volume at risk for recurrence using IMRT with image guidance [UM-B 2 of 4].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy (SABR) for Limited Metastases

trial_data.trial_name: SABR-COMET phase II trial (Palma DA, et al. Lancet 2019;393:2051-2058; J Clin Oncol 2020;38:2830-2838); Prospective registry (Chalkidou A, et al. Lancet Oncol 2021;22:98-106); Phase II study of SBRT + IL-2 in melanoma (Curti B, et al. J Immunother Cancer 2020;8:e000773)
trial_data.notes: SABR-COMET: 5-year OS 42.3% vs 17.7% with standard palliation. Registry: 1-year OS 92.3%, 2-year OS 79.2%. Melanoma-specific data from SBRT+IL-2 study.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Stereotactic ablative radiation therapy of oligometastases. Doses: 16–24 Gy/1 fraction or 24–60 Gy/2–8 fractions. Prescribed to appropriate target volume using SBRT techniques with image guidance. Extrapolated from studies in melanoma, kidney, and other oligometastatic cancers [UM-B 3 of 4].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Selective Internal Radiotherapy (SIRT) with Yttrium-90 for Liver Metastases
tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Consider as one or more liver-directed therapies (further study required)

trial_data.trial_name: Retrospective studies and one prospective phase II trial (Gonsalves CF, et al. Radiology 2019;293:223-231); Systematic review (Jia Z, et al. Eur J Radiol 2017;92:111-115)
trial_data.os_median: 18.5 months (treatment-naive group A); 19.2 months (post-immunoembolization group B) in phase II
trial_data.pfs_median: 8.1 months (hepatic PFS group A); 5.2 months (group B)
trial_data.orr: 39% (group A); 33% (group B) in phase II trial
trial_data.notes: Systematic review: median DCR at 3 months 73.6%, median OS 10 months, 1-year survival 34.6%. Treatment-related complications mild and uncommon.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Selective internal RT for patients with liver metastases using yttrium-90 microspheres. Further study required to determine appropriate patients and risks/benefits. Part of liver-directed therapy options for hepatic-dominant metastatic uveal melanoma [UM-B 3 of 4].

tier_1_regimens.site: Distant metastases from uveal melanoma (bone, brain, etc.)

trial_data.trial_name: Nonrandomized study (Huguenin PU, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1998;41:401-405)
trial_data.notes: Pain relief from bone metastases in 65% of patients, duration 2.4 months. Neurologic improvement in 55% for median 2.5 months. Short RT courses used (predominantly 5 × 4 Gy or 10 × 3 Gy).

tier_1_regimens.notes: For symptom palliation, photon beam radiotherapy can be used for treatment of distant metastases at risk for causing symptoms or for palliation of symptomatic distant metastases. Doses: 8–30 Gy in 1–10 fractions prescribed to appropriate target volume using appropriate 3D or IMRT techniques with or without image guidance [UM-B 3 of 4].

tier_2_handbook.definition: Uveal melanoma is the most common primary intraocular malignancy in adults, arising from melanocytes within the uveal tract, which comprises the choroid, ciliary body, and iris [1]. The NCCN guidelines specifically address melanomas of the choroid and ciliary body, with iris melanoma excluded due to its distinct clinical behavior, lower metastatic rate (~5% at 5 years), different AJCC staging, and unique molecular signature [2,5,13-18,20-25]. Approximately 85% of uveal melanomas originate in the choroid, with the remainder arising in the ciliary body [2]. The tumor typically presents as a pigmented lesion: in one large series of 8033 eyes, 55% were pigmented, 15% nonpigmented, and 30% mixed [130]. Morphologically, 75% are dome-shaped, while ~20% exhibit a mushroom shape due to rupture of Bruch's membrane; a small subset present as diffuse, flat, or multinodular lesions [130]. Subretinal fluid is present in 75% of cases [130]. Common symptoms include blurred vision, visual field defects, photopsia, floaters, and metamorphopsia, but 13–30% of patients are asymptomatic at diagnosis, discovered on routine eye examination [58,129,131-133]. Ciliary body melanomas are often hidden behind the iris, delaying diagnosis until the tumor is large; they may cause lens tilting, cataract, elevated intraocular pressure, and dilated episcleral sentinel vessels [28,132,141-143]. The most frequent site of metastasis is the liver (90%), followed by lung, bone, skin/soft tissue, and lymph nodes [10,311,375,390,391]. Biopsy is usually not necessary for initial diagnosis but can provide prognostic molecular information; if performed, molecular/chromosomal testing is preferred over cytology alone [e, UM-2A]. The diagnosis relies on comprehensive ophthalmic examination including biomicroscopy, dilated fundus exam, and adjunctive imaging such as ultrasonography, optical coherence tomography, and fluorescein angiography [60,142,148,149].

subtypes.name: Spindle cell melanoma
subtypes.description: Tumor composed of >90% spindle cells; classified as G1 by AJCC histologic grade.
subtypes.frequency: The majority of uveal melanomas have a spindle cell component; exact frequency not specified in source.
subtypes.key_associations: Lower metastatic risk compared to epithelioid-rich tumors; associated with better prognosis [ST-2].

subtypes.name: Mixed cell melanoma
subtypes.description: Tumor containing >10% epithelioid cells and <90% spindle cells; classified as G2.
subtypes.key_associations: Intermediate prognosis; epithelioid component confers higher metastatic potential [ST-2].

subtypes.name: Epithelioid cell melanoma
subtypes.description: Tumor composed of >90% epithelioid cells; classified as G3.
subtypes.frequency: Less common; exact frequency not specified.
subtypes.key_associations: Most aggressive histologic subtype; associated with higher risk of metastasis and worse survival [ST-2].

subtypes.name: Class 1 (low-risk) gene expression profile
subtypes.description: Gene expression profiling (15-GEP) class associated with low metastatic risk; further subdivided by PRAME expression.
subtypes.frequency: Class 1 tumors constitute a substantial proportion; exact frequency not specified in source.
subtypes.key_associations: Disomy 3, EIF1AX mutation, gain of chromosome 6p; PRAME expression modifies risk (Class 1 PRAME(+) is medium risk) [dd, UM-4].

subtypes.name: Class 2 (high-risk) gene expression profile
subtypes.description: Gene expression profiling class associated with high metastatic risk; PRAME expression may shorten time to metastasis.
subtypes.frequency: Class 2 tumors are less common but carry a 5- to 20-fold higher risk of metastasis than Class 1 [34,397-399].
subtypes.key_associations: Monosomy 3, gain of 8q, somatic BAP1 mutation; strongly associated with early metastasis [dd, UM-4, 398,401,412,417,418].

subtypes.name: BAP1-mutant uveal melanoma
subtypes.description: Tumors harboring somatic BAP1 mutations or deletions; also includes rare germline BAP1 variants (BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome).
subtypes.frequency: BAP1 mutation occurs in approximately 50% of uveal melanomas [398,401,412,417,418].
subtypes.key_associations: Class 2 profile, monosomy 3, high metastatic risk, early metastasis; germline mutations associated with familial uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, renal cell carcinoma, and other cancers [94-99].

subtypes.name: SF3B1-mutant uveal melanoma
subtypes.description: Tumors with recurrent mutations at codon 625 of SF3B1.
subtypes.frequency: Present in approximately 20% of uveal melanomas [25,51].
subtypes.key_associations: Associated with disomy 3, intermediate metastatic risk (medium risk), and late-onset metastases [51,420,421,424].

subtypes.name: EIF1AX-mutant uveal melanoma
subtypes.description: Tumors with recurrent somatic mutations in EIF1AX.
subtypes.frequency: Found in up to 20% of uveal melanomas [25,398,420-424].
subtypes.key_associations: Associated with disomy 3, low metastatic risk, and better prognosis [25,398,420-424].

subtypes.name: GNAQ/GNA11-mutant uveal melanoma
subtypes.description: Tumors harboring activating mutations in GNAQ or GNA11, which are early driver events.
subtypes.frequency: Mutations in GNAQ or GNA11 are found in >80% of uveal melanomas [23,47,48].
subtypes.key_associations: Activation of the MAPK pathway; these mutations are early and ubiquitous, not directly prognostic but required for tumorigenesis [23,47,48].

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Uveal melanoma is characterized by a distinct molecular pathogenesis that differs markedly from cutaneous melanoma. Early driver mutations occur in the GNAQ and GNA11 genes, which encode G-protein alpha subunits; these are found in >80% of uveal melanomas and lead to constitutive activation of the RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK signaling pathway [23,35,36,47,48,562]. Mutations in GNAQ and GNA11 are mutually exclusive and occur early in tumorigenesis, often present in benign nevus precursors [24]. Downstream, the most critical tumor-suppressor event is inactivation of BAP1 (BRCA1-associated protein 1), located on chromosome 3p21.1. Somatic BAP1 mutations or deletions occur in approximately 50% of uveal melanomas and are strongly associated with monosomy 3 and Class 2 gene expression profile, conferring high metastatic risk [49,50,398,401,412,417,418]. Germline BAP1 mutations predispose to the BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome, which includes uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, cutaneous melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, and other cancers [93-99]. Mutations in SF3B1 (splicing factor 3B subunit 1) are found in ~20% of uveal melanomas, typically in tumors with disomy 3, and are associated with intermediate metastatic risk and late-onset metastases [25,51,420,421,424]. EIF1AX mutations occur in up to 20% of cases, also in disomy 3 tumors, and are associated with low metastatic risk [25,398,420-424]. Chromosomal abnormalities are key molecular events: monosomy 3 is the most powerful prognostic marker, conferring high risk of metastasis, especially when combined with gain of chromosome 8q [13,34,45,398,400,401,403,407-413]. Gain of 6p is associated with low risk, while loss of 1p, 8p, and 16q may further modify risk [13,407,414-416]. PRAME (preferentially expressed antigen in melanoma) expression, present in about one-third of uveal melanomas, acts as a risk modifier within both Class 1 and Class 2 gene expression profiles; Class 1 PRAME-positive tumors carry medium risk, and Class 2 PRAME-positive tumors may have shorter time to metastasis [dd, 34,394-396,399,404,425]. BRAF, NRAS, KIT, and TERT promoter mutations are extremely rare in uveal melanoma, in contrast to cutaneous melanoma [23,35,36]. The cumulative effect of these genomic events drives a metastatic cascade that often involves early micrometastasis followed by variable latency [367].
tier_2_handbook.epidemiology: Uveal melanoma is the most common primary intraocular malignancy in adults, with an incidence of approximately 5 people per million annually in the United States [1]. The incidence distribution by sex, race, and geography differs from that of cutaneous melanoma [1]. Most uveal melanomas are localized at first presentation; fewer than 3% of patients present with detectable metastatic disease [3]. The risk of metastasis is highly stage-dependent: the 5-year risk ranges from 3% to 5% for stage I tumors to ≥44% for stage III tumors [4,5]. Large population-based analyses report disease-specific survival (DSS) between 70% and 81% at 5 years overall [4,6,7]. For patients with early-stage disease (stage I–II), 5-year DSS is ≥85% [4,6,7]. For those who develop distant metastatic disease, the prognosis is poor, with most studies reporting a 5-year survival rate of less than 20% [4,6,8-11]. Encouragingly, a small subset of patients with metastatic disease experience long-term survival (≥5 years) [8,10,375]. Importantly, the survival of patients with metastatic uveal melanoma has not improved over the past few decades [12]. The median overall survival after diagnosis of distant metastases varies widely across studies, from 3 to 30 months [10-12,454-456]. Uveal melanoma has a strong propensity for hematogenous dissemination, with the liver being the most common site of metastasis (90% of cases) [390]. Other metastatic sites include lung (20–30% of patients with metastases), bone (16–18%), skin/soft tissue (11–12%), lymph nodes (10–11%), and brain (4–5%) [10,235,374,375,391]. The disease is characterized by early micrometastasis often before primary treatment, followed by a variable latency period that can extend many years before overt metastases become clinically apparent [367]. Long-term follow-up studies show that the cumulative rate of distant metastasis continues to increase even beyond 10 years after initial treatment [259,317,375,388,389].

risk_factors.detail: Choroidal nevi occur in 1.9% to 6.5% of the population and can transform into choroidal melanoma. One study reported that 8% of uveal melanomas arose from a previously documented nevus [58,59]. The rate of transformation is debated; many small lesions presumed to be nevi may actually be melanoma [64,65]. Factors predictive of growth include symptoms, thickness >2 mm, diameter >5 mm, subretinal fluid, orange pigment, margin within 3 mm of optic disc, and ultrasound hollowness [61,66-73].
risk_factors.strength: Based on large population-based studies and case series; the association is well-established, though the exact transformation rate is uncertain.

risk_factors.detail: Hyperpigmentation of the episclera, uvea, and skin is much more common in patients with uveal melanoma than in the general population [83-88].
risk_factors.strength: Strong association based on case-control and cohort studies; relative risk not quantified in source.

risk_factors.factor: Familial uveal melanoma and hereditary syndromes
risk_factors.detail: Germline pathogenic variants in BAP1, PALB2, POT1, or MBD4 predispose to uveal melanoma [1-9]. BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome includes uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, cutaneous melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, and other cancers [93-99]. POT1 truncating variants implicate telomere dysfunction [107]. MBD4 mutations are associated with high tumor mutational burden [108,109].
risk_factors.strength: High; multiple gene discovery studies and family-based analyses confirm causality; routine genetic counseling is recommended for early-onset (<30 years) or multiple primary cancers [UM-A].

risk_factors.factor: Light skin color, propensity to sunburn, light eye (iris) color
risk_factors.detail: Lighter skin and eye color and a tendency to sunburn are associated with increased risk of uveal melanoma [10,112-115].
risk_factors.strength: Consistent across multiple meta-analyses and case-control studies; the effect size is moderate.

risk_factors.factor: Strong personal or family history of cancer
risk_factors.detail: A personal or family history of cancers associated with BAP1 (mesothelioma, renal cell, etc.), BRCA1/BRCA2/PALB2/POT1 (breast, ovarian, pancreatic), or Lynch syndrome (via MBD4 or other mismatch repair genes) increases risk [11, b, UM-A]. Early age of diagnosis (<30 years) is a red flag.
risk_factors.strength: Based on case-control and population-based studies; genetic testing is indicated if features of hereditary syndrome are present.

risk_factors.detail: Welding has been associated with increased risk of uveal melanoma, and a dose-response relationship with job duration has been reported [12,116].
risk_factors.strength: Evidence from case-control studies; the absolute risk increase is modest.

risk_factors.detail: The presence of cutaneous melanoma does not increase the risk of uveal melanoma. Routine screening for uveal melanoma in cutaneous melanoma patients is not required [UM-A, 121-125].
risk_factors.strength: Most large population-based studies show no relationship; contrary findings are not robust.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Uveal melanoma typically presents as a pigmented lesion arising in the ciliary body or choroid, most commonly in the choroid (~85%) [2]. The lesion is pigmented in approximately 55% of cases, nonpigmented in 15%, and mixed in 30% [130]. Morphologically, 75% present as dome-shaped tumors, while 20% develop a mushroom shape due to rupture of Bruch’s membrane; a small number are diffuse, flat, plateau-shaped, or have a multinodular tapioca appearance [130]. Subretinal fluid is present in 75% of patients at diagnosis [130]. Ciliary body melanomas are often hidden behind the iris, leading to delayed detection; they may cause lens tilting, cataract, elevated intraocular pressure, and dilated episcleral sentinel vessels [141-143]. The majority of patients are symptomatic, but studies report 13%–30% are asymptomatic and discovered on routine eye examination [58,129,131-133]. Real-world data indicate high rates of delayed diagnosis (23%–37%) even among symptomatic patients [58,132,133]. The most common symptom is blurred vision; other symptoms include visual field loss, photopsia, metamorphopsia, floaters, irritation, pain, and redness [58,129,131]. The disease is distinct from cutaneous melanoma: BRAF, NRAS, KIT, and TERT promoter mutations are extremely rare in uveal melanoma, whereas GNAQ/GNA11 mutations occur in >80% of cases [23,35,36]. Risk of metastasis varies by stage: 5-year risk ranges from 3%–5% for stage I to ≥44% for stage III [4,5]. Overall 5-year disease-specific survival (DSS) is 70%–81%; for stage I–II, DSS ≥85% [4,6,7]. For those with distant metastatic disease, 5-year survival is <20% [4,6,8-11].

symptoms.name: Blurred vision
symptoms.detail: Most common symptom; may be due to tumor-induced retinal detachment or direct compression of the macula
symptoms.frequency: Present in majority of symptomatic patients; specific percentage not stated

symptoms.name: Visual field defect/loss
symptoms.detail: Often described as a shadow or curtain over part of the visual field
symptoms.alarm: Yes, may indicate large tumor or retinal detachment

symptoms.name: Photopsia
symptoms.detail: Flashes of light, possibly from retinal irritation
symptoms.frequency: Reported in patients with symptomatic uveal melanoma

symptoms.name: Floaters
symptoms.detail: Mobile opacities in the vitreous, may indicate tumor shedding or hemorrhage

symptoms.name: Metamorphopsia
symptoms.detail: Distortion of vision, often due to subretinal fluid

symptoms.name: Irritation and pain
symptoms.detail: May indicate secondary glaucoma or ciliary body involvement

symptoms.name: Asymptomatic
symptoms.detail: Discovered on routine eye exam; 13%–30% of patients are asymptomatic at diagnosis [58,129,131-133]
symptoms.alarm: Low; but any pigmented lesion requires assessment

signs.name: Pigmented intraocular mass
signs.detail: Lesion color: pigmented in 55%, nonpigmented in 15%, mixed in 30% [130]; shape: dome (75%), mushroom (20%), diffuse/flat (5%) [130]
signs.frequency: ~85% of uveal melanomas arise in choroid; remainder in ciliary body [2]

signs.name: Subretinal fluid
signs.detail: Present in majority of patients, often leads to retinal detachment; detected by ophthalmoscopy, OCT, or ultrasound [130]

signs.name: Orange pigment (lipofuscin)
signs.detail: Highly autofluorescent; associated with risk of growth in small melanocytic lesions [62,72]
signs.frequency: Common in melanomas; specific frequency not given for all stages

signs.name: Ciliary body involvement signs
signs.detail: Sentinel episcleral vessels, lens tilt/displacement, cataract, elevated intraocular pressure [141-143]
signs.frequency: More common in ciliary body melanomas (hidden location)

signs.name: Extraocular extension
signs.detail: Detectable by imaging (MRI, ultrasound) or intraoperatively; present in ~3% at diagnosis, up to 12% of T4 tumors [7,130,224,358,359]

atypical_or_missed: Diffuse, flat, or plateau-shaped tumors that may be mistaken for choroidal nevi [130]
Multinodular tapioca appearance, rare but easily overlooked [130]
Amelanotic tumors that appear nonpigmented and may be confused with metastases, hemangiomas, or other lesions [d]
Ciliary body melanoma hidden behind the iris, often missed until large [28,132,141,142]
Patients with minimal symptoms or asymptomatic presentation leading to delayed diagnosis (23%–37% of cases) [58,132,133]

red_flags: Presence of symptoms (vision loss, blurred vision, photopsia, metamorphopsia, floaters) [f]
Tumor thickness >2 mm and/or largest basal diameter >5 mm [f]
Tumor margin within 3 mm of optic disc [f]
Documented growth of a melanocytic choroidal lesion [f]

Risk factors for development: choroidal nevi, ocular/oculodermal melanocytosis, familial uveal melanoma (germline pathogenic variants in BAP1, PALB2, POT1, MBD4), light skin/eye color, propensity to sunburn, strong personal/family history of cancer (especially BAP1-associated cancers: mesothelioma, cutaneous melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, cholangiocarcinoma, meningioma; BRCA1/BRCA2, PALB2, POT1: breast, ovarian, pancreatic cancers), occupational history of welding [UM-A]
Ciliary body melanoma signs: sentinel episcleral vessels, lens displacement, cataract, elevated intraocular pressure [141-143]
Extraocular extension detected on imaging or intraoperatively [7,130,224,358,359]

diagnostic.rationale: Initial detection and characterization of suspicious pigmented lesion; includes measurement of visual acuity, examination of anterior and posterior segments
diagnostic.key_findings: Identify lesion location, size, shape, color, subretinal fluid, orange pigment, ciliary body involvement; full dilation essential [60,148,149]

diagnostic.rationale: Allows visualization of posterior uveal melanomas and detection of features such as subretinal fluid, orange pigment, and tumor margins
diagnostic.key_findings: Most choroidal melanomas detectable with good pupil dilation [60]

diagnostic.rationale: Document lesion borders, shape, basal diameter, area; detect orange pigment, drusen, halo [151,152]
diagnostic.key_findings: Useful for monitoring growth; not sufficient alone for differential diagnosis [153]

diagnostic.rationale: One of the most useful tools; A-scan shows low to medium internal reflectivity (positive angle kappa sign), B-scan shows acoustically hollow mass with choroidal excavation and orbital shadowing [135,148,154,159]
diagnostic.key_findings: Melanoma: low reflectivity, regular internal structure, solid consistency, vascularization signs; distinguishes from metastases (high reflectivity) and hemangiomas [148,159,160]

diagnostic.rationale: High-resolution imaging of anterior segment tumors, especially ciliary body involvement [UM-1]
diagnostic.key_findings: Better resolution of anterior anatomy than OCT for posterior margin [177]

diagnostic.rationale: Detect orange pigment (lipofuscin) which is highly autofluorescent; distinguish from drusen [163,165,166]
diagnostic.key_findings: Melanoma shows slight intrinsic hyperautofluorescence; orange pigment highly autofluorescent; melanocytoma shows hypofluorescence [163,168]

diagnostic.rationale: Evaluate retina and RPE changes; detect subretinal/intraretinal fluid, shaggy photoreceptors, structural loss; enhanced depth imaging (EDI-OCT) useful for small lesions [162,170]
diagnostic.key_findings: Subretinal fluid, lipofuscin, RPE atrophy more common in melanoma than nevi; optical density ratio may distinguish from metastases [175]

diagnostic.rationale: Characterize lesion vasculature; detect 'double circulation' (intrinsic tumor circulation) [141,178]
diagnostic.key_findings: Helps distinguish melanoma from nevi, hemorrhages; also useful for detection of radiation retinopathy after treatment [148]

diagnostic.rationale: Used when other methods inadequate (e.g., large ciliary body tumors, cataracts); may overestimate size [181]
diagnostic.key_findings: Detect and measure tumors obscured by media opacities [60,180]

diagnostic.test: MRI of orbit (with and without IV contrast)
diagnostic.rationale: Used when diagnosis uncertain, for large tumors near optic nerve, or suspicion of extraocular extension; helpful for RT planning [148,182-186]
diagnostic.key_findings: Uveal melanoma: high T1 signal, low T2 signal; distinguishes from hemangiomas (hyperintense T2) [60,141]

diagnostic.rationale: Usually not needed for initial diagnosis; may confirm diagnosis in uncertain cases (amelanotic tumors, retinal detachment) or for prognostic molecular analysis [d,e]
diagnostic.key_findings: FNAB can yield cytologic diagnosis; molecular/chromosomal testing preferred over cytology alone [e]; risks include hemorrhage, retinal detachment, tumor seeding (rare) [191-196]

staging.test: Ocular imaging (MRI orbit with/without IV contrast)
staging.rationale: If not previously done for large tumors, proximity to nerve, or suspected extraocular extension; to confirm extraocular extension and for treatment planning [UM-2]
staging.indication: Large tumor, close to nerve, suspicion of extraocular involvement

staging.rationale: Provide prognostic information for risk stratification and clinical trial eligibility [e, UM-2]
staging.indication: All patients with confirmed uveal melanoma, after careful discussion of risks/benefits

staging.rationale: Screen for distant metastases before primary treatment; most common site is liver [i]
staging.indication: All patients; for small low-risk tumors, may defer until after primary treatment

staging.test: Contrast-enhanced MRI of liver (preferred) or ultrasound
staging.rationale: Liver is most frequent metastasis site; MRI preferred for sensitivity [i, ff]
staging.indication: Baseline screening; modality determined by institutional expertise

staging.rationale: Assess for extrahepatic metastases; performed with IV contrast unless contraindicated [i]

staging.test: Chest imaging (CT without contrast or chest x-ray)
staging.indication: Part of surveillance imaging for distant disease

biomarkers.rationale: Class 2 associated with 5- to 20-fold higher risk of metastasis than Class 1 [203,397-399]; PRAME expression risk modifier [dd]
biomarkers.when: At time of biopsy of primary tumor; preferred over cytology alone [e]
biomarkers.mandatory: Not mandatory but strongly recommended for risk stratification

biomarkers.rationale: Risk modifier to 15-GEP; associated with increased metastasis risk in Class 1 and Class 2 tumors [dd, 396]

biomarkers.test: Chromosomal analysis: monosomy 3, gain of 8q, disomy 3, gain of 6p
biomarkers.rationale: Monosomy 3 and 8q gain portend high risk; disomy 3 and 6p gain indicate low risk [34,398,400,401]
biomarkers.when: From biopsy specimen; preferred over cytology alone

biomarkers.rationale: BAP1 mutation (high risk), SF3B1 (medium risk, late metastases), EIF1AX (low risk) [25,51,398,401,420,421]
biomarkers.when: From biopsy; somatic BAP1 mutation is a high-risk feature [UM-4]

biomarkers.rationale: Required to determine eligibility for tebentafusp-tebn therapy in metastatic uveal melanoma [ll]
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis of distant metastatic disease
biomarkers.mandatory: Yes, for metastatic patients to guide therapy

biomarkers.rationale: May assist in determining prognosis in the setting of metastatic disease [mm]
biomarkers.when: Consider at diagnosis of distant metastasis

biomarkers.rationale: Evaluate for hereditary syndromes (BAP1, PALB2, POT1, MBD4) if early age (<30), personal/family history of other cancers [UM-A]
biomarkers.when: In patients with risk factors for hereditary uveal melanoma

do_not_miss: Biopsy of primary tumor for prognostic analysis (strongly consider) [e]
HLA-A*02:01 testing in all patients with distant metastatic disease [ll]
Assessment of ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension [UM-2]
Documentation of tumor dimensions, distance from disc/fovea, subretinal fluid, orange pigment [UM-1]
Consideration of hereditary syndrome evaluation (BAP1, PALB2, POT1, MBD4) in appropriate patients [UM-A]
avoid: Routine CNS imaging (brain MRI) in asymptomatic patients; only indicated if neurologic symptoms present [ii]
Routine use of color fundus photography alone for differential diagnosis [153]
Over-reliance on LFTs for early detection of liver metastases due to poor sensitivity [z, 374]
Routine screening for uveal melanoma in patients with cutaneous melanoma (no increased risk) [UM-A]
Plaque brachytherapy for tumors with largest basal diameter >19 mm or thickness >10 mm (insufficient coverage) [j, o]

staging.system: AJCC 8th edition (2017) for choroidal and ciliary body melanoma; iris melanoma has separate staging and is not covered in this guideline [20]
