No evidence of primary tumor
Tumor size category 1 (see Figure 1 for thickness/diameter matrix)
Tumor size category 1 without ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension
Tumor size category 1 with ciliary body involvement
Tumor size category 1 without ciliary body involvement but with extraocular extension ≤5 mm in largest diameter
Tumor size category 1 with ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension ≤5 mm in largest diameter
Tumor size category 2
Tumor size category 2 without ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension
Tumor size category 2 with ciliary body involvement
Tumor size category 2 without ciliary body involvement but with extraocular extension ≤5 mm in largest diameter
Tumor size category 2 with ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension ≤5 mm in largest diameter
Tumor size category 3
Tumor size category 3 without ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension
Tumor size category 3 with ciliary body involvement
Tumor size category 3 without ciliary body involvement but with extraocular extension ≤5 mm in largest diameter
Tumor size category 3 with ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension ≤5 mm in largest diameter
Tumor size category 4
Tumor size category 4 without ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension
Tumor size category 4 with ciliary body involvement
Tumor size category 4 without ciliary body involvement but with extraocular extension ≤5 mm in largest diameter
Tumor size category 4 with ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension ≤5 mm in largest diameter
Any tumor size category with extraocular extension >5 mm in largest diameter

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed
Regional lymph node metastases or discrete tumor deposits in the orbit
Metastasis in one or more regional lymph node(s)
No regional lymph nodes are positive, but there are discrete tumor deposits in the orbit not contiguous to the eye (choroidal and ciliary body)

m_stages.description: Largest diameter of the largest metastasis ≤3.0 cm
Largest diameter of the largest metastasis 3.1–8.0 cm
Largest diameter of the largest metastasis ≥8.1 cm

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor size category 1 without ciliary body involvement or extraocular extension, no nodal or distant metastases
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 5-year DSS ≥85% (stage I–II combined); 5-year risk of metastasis 3%–5% [4,5]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; primary treatment options include brachytherapy, particle beam RT, observation in select patients, or enucleation

stage_groups.criteria: T1b-d, N0, M0 OR T2a, N0, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor size 1 with ciliary body involvement or small extraocular extension, or tumor size 2 without those features

stage_groups.criteria: T2b, N0, M0 OR T3a, N0, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor size 2 with ciliary body involvement, or tumor size 3 without ciliary body involvement or extraocular extension
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 5-year DSS ≥85% (stage I–II); risk of metastasis higher than stage I

stage_groups.criteria: T2c-d, N0, M0 OR T3b-c, N0, M0 OR T4a, N0, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor size 2 with extraocular extension (≤5 mm), or tumor size 3 with ciliary body involvement or small extraocular extension, or tumor size 4 without other features
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 5-year risk of metastasis ≥44% for stage III overall [4,5]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative, but high risk of distant metastasis; intensified surveillance

stage_groups.criteria: T3d, N0, M0 OR T4b-c, N0, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor size 3 with ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension ≤5 mm, or tumor size 4 with ciliary body involvement or extraocular extension ≤5 mm
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: High risk; specific survival not separately reported
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; consider adjuvant RT for margin-positive cases

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor size 4 with ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension ≤5 mm, or any tumor size with extraocular extension >5 mm
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; often requires enucleation and/or orbital exenteration

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, N1, M0 OR Any T, Any N, M1a-c
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Regional lymph node metastases or discrete orbital tumor deposits, or any distant metastasis (M1a-c)
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 5-year survival <20% [4,6,8-11]; median OS for metastatic disease 3–30 months [10-12,454-456]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative; clinical trial preferred; consider liver-directed therapies and systemic therapy

staging_pearls: Tumor size categories 1–4 are defined by a matrix of largest basal diameter (mm) and thickness (mm) as shown in Figure 1 (ST-3) [ST-3]
Ciliary body involvement and extraocular extension (size ≤5 mm vs >5 mm) modify T stage and carry independent prognostic significance [ST-1]
Histologic grade (G1–G3) is recorded but not used for staging; spindle cell (G1) has better prognosis, epithelioid (G3) worse [ST-2]
AJCC staging is complemented by molecular risk stratification (GEP, chromosome 3 status, BAP1 mutations) for follow-up surveillance intensity [UM-4]
Iris melanoma has a separate AJCC staging system and is excluded from these recommendations; it has a lower metastatic rate [5,14-18]
The stage groups incorporate only T, N, M; histologic grade and molecular markers are not included in the AJCC stage group [ST-2]
Extraocular extension >5 mm automatically upgrades to T4e regardless of tumor size category [ST-1]

For clinical practice, largest basal diameter can be estimated in optic disc diameters (1 DD ≈ 1.5 mm), thickness in diopters (2.5 D ≈ 1 mm); ultrasonography and fundus photography provide accurate measurements [ST-1]
Patients with stage I–II have low to intermediate risk of metastasis; stage III have high risk (≥44% at 5 years); stage IV have very poor prognosis [4,5]
The 5-year DSS for early-stage (I–II) is ≥85%; overall DSS for all stages is 70%–81% [4,6,7]

management_principles.overview: Uveal melanoma is the most common primary intraocular malignancy in adults, with an incidence of approximately 5 people per million annually in the United States [1]. Most uveal melanomas are localized at first presentation, and <3% present with metastases [3]. The treatment philosophy emphasizes a multimodal approach tailored to tumor size, location, extraocular extension, and patient factors, with the dual goals of local control (preferably with globe preservation) and risk mitigation for distant metastasis. For localized disease, plaque brachytherapy, particle beam therapy, stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS), and enucleation are definitive options. For metastatic disease, clinical trial enrollment is preferred; when not available, liver-directed therapies (e.g., percutaneous hepatic perfusion, embolization, ablation, resection/RT) are recommended for hepatic-dominant disease, and systemic therapy including tebentafusp-tebn for HLA-A*02:01-positive patients or immune checkpoint inhibitors for others [UM-6, UMSYS-1]. The panel strongly recommends referral to centers with expertise in uveal melanoma management [UM-1 footnote a]. Multidisciplinary care is essential, involving an ophthalmic oncologist, radiation oncologist, medical oncologist, pathologist, and brachytherapy physicist [3]. Performance status is not formally defined in the guideline, but clinical trials supporting recommendations enrolled patients with ECOG performance status 0–1 [535].

by_intent.patient_group: Localized primary uveal melanoma (choroid or ciliary body, no metastasis)
by_intent.approach: Definitive local therapy: brachytherapy, particle beam therapy, SRS, or enucleation based on tumor size (large/medium/small) and patient factors. For patients with extraocular extension, additional local control measures (observation, local excision, RT to orbit, orbital exenteration) are considered [UM-2, UM-3].

by_intent.patient_group: Metastatic disease with limited hepatic burden
by_intent.approach: Clinical trial preferred; if not, liver-directed therapies (percutaneous hepatic perfusion, embolization, ablative procedures, resection/RT) ± systemic therapy. Systemic options include tebentafusp-tebn for HLA-A*02:01-positive (category 1) [526], ipilimumab + nivolumab (preferred), other checkpoint inhibitors, and cytotoxic agents [UMSYS-1].

by_intent.patient_group: Metastatic disease not amenable to local therapy
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy as above, with best supportive/palliative care according to NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care [UM-6].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Management of uveal melanoma requires a multidisciplinary team including an ophthalmic oncologist, radiation oncologist, medical oncologist, pathologist, and brachytherapy physicist [3]. Plaque brachytherapy should be performed by an experienced team that includes an ophthalmic oncologist, radiation oncologist, and brachytherapy physicist [UM-B 1 of 4]. Particle beam therapy similarly requires a multidisciplinary team including ophthalmic oncologist, radiation oncologist, and particle beam physicist [UM-B 2 of 4]. Referral to centers with expertise in uveal melanoma is recommended upon clinical presentation [UM-1 footnote a].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: The NCCN guideline does not provide a formal performance status guide for treatment selection. Clinical trials that support systemic therapy recommendations, such as the phase II GEM-1402 trial of nivolumab plus ipilimumab, required ECOG performance status 0–1 [535]. In practice, performance status is considered in treatment decisions, particularly for combination immunotherapy and liver-directed therapies.

management_pathways.title: Observation for Uncertain Diagnosis (Small Lesions <3 Risk Factors)
branching_basis: Number of risk factors for growth (<3)

nodes.criteria: Suspicious pigmented uveal lesion with uncertain diagnosis (especially for small tumors) and/or <3 risk factors for growth (presence of symptoms, thickness >2 mm, diameter >5 mm, subretinal fluid, orange pigment, margin within 3 mm of optic disc, ultrasound hollowness) [UM-1].

options.label: Observe and re-evaluate for growth or features of malignancy every 2–6 months as clinically indicated, then close follow-up for 5 years, then annually thereafter [UM-1].

management_pathways.title: Primary Treatment for Small Tumors (Largest Diameter 5–19 mm, Thickness <2.5 mm)

nodes.criteria: Tumor with largest diameter 5–19 mm and thickness <2.5 mm [UM-2].

options.label: Regular ophthalmologic follow-up in select patients
Other options in highly select patients: laser treatment or enucleation (per patient preference) for those not good candidates for brachytherapy or particle beam

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy after primary treatment.

management_pathways.title: Primary Treatment for Medium Tumors (Largest Diameter ≤19 mm, Thickness 2.5–10 mm)

nodes.criteria: Tumor with largest diameter ≤19 mm and thickness 2.5–10 mm [UM-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: If concern that adequate response was not achieved from initial radiation, consider additional treatment with resection, laser ablation, transpupillary thermotherapy, or cryotherapy [UM-2 footnote s]. Enucleation is recommended for patients with neovascular glaucoma, tumor replacing >50% of globe, or blind, painful eyes; consider for extensive extraocular extension [UM-2 footnote t]. For limited extraocular extension, brachytherapy or particle beam radiation is appropriate [UM-2 footnote p].

management_pathways.title: Primary Treatment for Large Tumors (Largest Diameter >19 mm or Thickness >10 mm or Thickness >8 mm with Optic Nerve Involvement)

nodes.criteria: Tumor with largest diameter >19 mm (any thickness) or thickness >10 mm (any diameter) or thickness >8 mm with optic nerve involvement (any diameter) [UM-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Enucleation is recommended for patients with neovascular glaucoma, tumor replacing >50% of globe, or blind, painful eyes; consider for extensive extraocular extension [UM-2 footnote t]. Pathologic evaluation per CAP synoptic protocol [UM-2 footnote u]. For inadequate response to RT, consider additional treatment with resection, laser ablation, transpupillary thermotherapy, or cryotherapy [UM-2 footnote s].

management_pathways.title: Additional Primary Treatment for Extraocular Extension at Enucleation

nodes.criteria: Microscopically positive or close margins after enucleation but no clinical, intraoperative, or radiographic evidence of gross residual disease in the orbit [UM-3].
Visible extraocular tumor (biopsy extraocular tissue if possible) [UM-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Local control options based on extent of disease.

management_pathways.title: Risk-Based Surveillance for Distant Metastasis After Primary Treatment
branching_basis: Risk stratification: low, medium, high based on genetic markers and AJCC T stage

nodes.criteria: Low risk: Class 1A or Class 1 PRAME(-), disomy 3, gain of chromosome 6p, EIF1AX mutation, T1 (AJCC) [UM-4].

options.label: Imaging to evaluate signs or symptoms as clinically indicated; consider surveillance imaging every 12 months for 5 years, or as clinically indicated [UM-4].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Standard follow-up for affected eye with color fundus photography and ultrasonography every 3–6 months for 3–5 years, then every 6–12 months if stable [UM-4 footnote x].

nodes.criteria: Medium risk: Class 1B or Class 1 PRAME(+), SF3B1 mutation, T2 and T3 (AJCC) [UM-4].

options.label: Imaging to evaluate signs or symptoms; consider surveillance imaging every 6–12 months for 10 years, then as clinically indicated [UM-4].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Same eye follow-up schedule as low risk.

nodes.criteria: High risk: Class 2, monosomy 3, gain of chromosome 8q, BAP1 somatic mutation, T4 (AJCC) [UM-4].

options.label: Imaging to evaluate signs or symptoms; consider surveillance imaging every 3–6 months for 5 years, then every 6–12 months for years 6–10, then as clinically indicated [UM-4].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Same eye follow-up schedule; additional risk factors: juxtapapillary location and ciliary body involvement [UM-4 footnote cc].

management_pathways.title: Treatment for Ocular Recurrence
branching_basis: Intraocular recurrence (limited to eye, no orbital involvement)
Extraocular involvement/orbital involvement after prior enucleation

nodes.criteria: Intraocular recurrence (limited to eye, no orbital involvement) [UM-5].

options.label: Laser ablation (or transpupillary thermotherapy for small recurrences when RT/surgery not possible)

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy after treatment of recurrence.

options.label: Surgical resection (partial orbital tumor resection, enucleation, or exenteration) ± RT to orbit ± cryotherapy
Surgical resection or cryotherapy to orbital tumor

adjuvant_logic.summary: May combine surgical resection with RT and/or cryotherapy.

options.label: Consider one or more: regional isolation perfusion (melphalan percutaneous hepatic perfusion [PHP]), embolization (chemo-, radio-, immuno-), ablative procedures (thermal ablation, cryotherapy), resection and/or RT [UM-6].
Best supportive/palliative care (NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care)

adjuvant_logic.summary: After treatment, image to assess response; if no evidence of disease, consider observation or clinical trial for adjuvant therapy; if residual/progressive disease, try other options.

options.label: Best supportive/palliative care (NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Same response assessment and follow-up as hepatic-dominant.

items.text: Complete history and physical (H&P), including personal/family history of prior or current cancers (outside the eye) [UM-1].
items.recommendation: Perform for all patients with suspicious pigmented uveal lesion.

items.text: Comprehensive eye exam: biomicroscopy (front and back of eye), dilated fundus exam (indirect ophthalmoscopy), measure visual acuity [UM-1].
Measure and document tumor location, size (diameter, thickness), distance from disc and fovea, ciliary body involvement, subretinal fluid, orange pigment [UM-1].
items.recommendation: Required for staging and treatment planning.

items.text: Color fundus photography, ocular ultrasound (including ultrasound biomicroscopy for anterior/ciliary body lesions), autofluorescence of ocular fundus, optical coherence tomography (OCT), retinal fluorescein angiography, transillumination, MRI (with and without IV contrast) as clinically indicated [UM-1].
items.recommendation: Additional options to confirm diagnosis and characterize tumor.

items.text: Baseline extraocular imaging to screen for distant disease: at minimum, contrast-enhanced MRI (preferred) or ultrasound of the liver; additional imaging may include chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast [UM-2 footnote i].
items.recommendation: All patients should have liver imaging prior to treatment; modality preference determined by institutional expertise.

items.text: Consider biopsy if needed to confirm diagnosis (e.g., amelanotic tumors, retinal detachment) [UM-1 footnote d].
items.recommendation: Biopsy is usually not necessary for initial diagnosis and first-line treatment, but may be useful in uncertain cases.

items.text: Strongly consider biopsy of primary tumor for prognostic analysis (molecular/chromosomal testing preferred over cytology alone) [UM-2].
items.recommendation: Provides information for risk stratification and clinical trial eligibility; risks/benefits should be carefully discussed.

items.text: For patients with distant metastatic disease: HLA-A*02:01 testing [UM-6 footnote ll].
items.recommendation: Required to determine eligibility for tebentafusp-tebn therapy.

items.text: Consider liver function tests (LFTs), including LDH and alkaline phosphatase, for prognostic value in metastatic disease [UM-6 footnote mm].

Assess risk factors for developing uveal melanoma: choroidal nevi, ocular/oculodermal melanocytosis, familial uveal melanoma (germline pathogenic variants in BAP1, PALB2, POT1, MBD4), light skin/eye color, strong personal/family history of cancer, occupational welding history [UM-A 1 of 2].
items.recommendation: Evaluate for hereditary syndrome and refer for genetic counseling/testing if indicated (early age <30 years, history of other primary cancers, family history of associated cancers).

surgery.role: Surgery plays a central role in the management of uveal melanoma, primarily as enucleation for large tumors or when globe-conserving options are not feasible or have failed. Less commonly, orbital exenteration is used for extensive extraocular extension. Local excision may be considered for small ciliary body tumors (<3 clock hours) [UM-2 footnote r]. Enucleation is also indicated for local recurrence not amenable to radiation or laser, and for blind painful eyes. Pathologic evaluation of enucleated specimens should follow the College of American Pathologists uveal melanoma synoptic report [UM-2 footnote u].
principles: Enucleation is a standard treatment for large uveal melanomas and provides excellent local control with very low recurrence risk (~1% in COMS) [234-236].
Pre-enucleation radiation is not recommended; the COMS trial showed no survival benefit [234,245,246].

Enucleation involves complete removal of the eye with insertion of an orbital implant; both porous and nonporous implants have similar outcomes, though higher ptosis with acrylic and greater need for ocularist treatment with hydroxyapatite [244].
For extraocular extension, surgical options include local excision, orbital exenteration, or enucleation with adjuvant RT to orbit [UM-3].
Resection and/or RT are also used for limited hepatic metastases [UM-6].

procedures.name: Enucleation
procedures.approach: Complete removal of the globe; orbital implant placed to allow prosthetic fitting. Standardized technique with minimal manipulation to avoid tumor spillage [141,241-243].
procedures.indications: Large tumors (>19 mm diameter or >10 mm thickness or >8 mm thickness with optic nerve involvement) when radiation not preferred; neovascular glaucoma; tumor replacing >50% of globe; blind, painful eye; patient preference; extensive extraocular extension; intraocular recurrence not suitable for radiation [UM-2, UM-5].
procedures.key_complications: Pain, hemorrhage, nausea/vomiting, cardiovascular/pulmonary problems, urinary retention, fever, local surgical issues, implant extrusion or infection. Long-term: ptosis, need for prosthesis adjustment [234,244].

procedures.name: Orbital Exenteration
procedures.approach: Removal of globe and adjacent orbital contents. Used in <1% of patients [14]; about 2.5% of enucleation patients require exenteration [362].
procedures.indications: Extensive extraocular extension, orbital recurrence after enucleation, or gross orbital disease not amenable to local excision [UM-3, UM-5].
procedures.key_complications: Significant disfigurement, loss of orbital contents, infection, bleeding. Value debated due to poor outcomes in some studies [363-365].

procedures.name: Local Excision (Resection)
procedures.approach: Surgical removal of tumor with preservation of the eye; can be transscleral or endoresection. Limited data; used for small ciliary body tumors (<3 clock hours) or as adjunct to radiation [UM-2 footnote r].
procedures.indications: Small ciliary body tumors; extraocular extension; local recurrence [UM-3, UM-5].
procedures.key_complications: Higher local recurrence rate compared to enucleation or radiation; risk of retinal detachment, hemorrhage, vitreous loss [237-240].

surgery.resectability_criteria: No formal resectability criteria are provided in the NCCN guideline. For hepatic metastases, complete resection is associated with best outcomes when achievable [455,467]. The decision for surgical management depends on tumor size, location, presence of extraocular extension, patient fitness, and local expertise. Enucleation is recommended for tumors exceeding brachytherapy plaque size limits (largest basal diameter >19 mm) or when globe salvage is not possible.

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy is the most common definitive treatment for primary uveal melanoma, with plaque brachytherapy, particle beam therapy, and stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) constituting primary options. For orbital involvement, fractionated photon radiotherapy is used as adjuvant to surgery. Metastasis-directed radiotherapy includes stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR) for oligometastases and palliative RT for symptomatic metastases.
principles: Plaque brachytherapy is appropriate for tumors ≤19 mm largest basal diameter and ≤10 mm thickness; may be used selectively for larger tumors [UM-B 1 of 4].
Particle beam therapy is a common definitive RT; a prospective trial found no difference in cause-specific survival between plaque and particle beam for tumors ≤15 mm diameter and ≤11 mm height [8].
SRS is least often used for primary tumors; prospective data limited [14,15].
Fractionated photon RT is preferred as adjuvant to surgery for orbital involvement (e.g., margin-positive enucleation) [UM-B 2 of 4].
Ablative RT for limited metastases uses SBRT; palliative RT for symptomatic metastases uses hypofractionated schedules [UM-B 3 of 4].
Treatment planning should involve multidisciplinary team (ophthalmic oncologist, radiation oncologist, physicist) [3,9].

dose_frameworks.name: Iodine-125 Plaque Brachytherapy (COMS)
dose_frameworks.schedule: Single application, plaque left in place until prescribed dose delivered
dose_frameworks.indication: Primary treatment for medium-sized uveal melanoma (2.5–10 mm apical height, ≤16 mm basal diameter) [227, UM-B 1 of 4].

dose_frameworks.name: Other Radioisotope Plaque (Ru-106, Pd-103, etc.)
dose_frameworks.schedule: Single application, dose prescribed to apex or minimum to base
dose_frameworks.indication: Primary treatment; margins may vary by isotope [UM-B 1 of 4].

dose_frameworks.name: Proton Beam Therapy (Intraocular Tumors)
dose_frameworks.schedule: Daily or every other day over 1–2 weeks
dose_frameworks.indication: Primary treatment for uveal melanoma; also for margin-positive enucleation, intraocular/orbital recurrence [9-12, UM-B 2 of 4].

dose_frameworks.name: Carbon Ion Beam Therapy (Intraocular Tumors)
dose_frameworks.indication: Primary treatment for unfavorably located or locally advanced choroidal melanoma [13, UM-B 2 of 4].

dose_frameworks.name: Fractionated Photon SRS (Intraocular Tumors)
dose_frameworks.indication: Primary or recurrent intraocular tumors [UM-B 2 of 4].

dose_frameworks.name: Single-Fraction SRS
dose_frameworks.indication: Primary or recurrent intraocular tumors [UM-B 2 of 4].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant Fractionated Photon RT (Orbit)
dose_frameworks.indication: Adjuvant to surgery for orbital involvement (margin-positive enucleation or exenteration, resected regional metastases) [18,19, UM-B 2 of 4].

dose_frameworks.name: Stereotactic Ablative RT (Metastases)
dose_frameworks.indication: Ablative treatment for limited (oligo-) metastases from uveal melanoma [20-23, UM-B 3 of 4].

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative RT (Symptomatic Metastases)
dose_frameworks.schedule: Variable, often daily or every other day
dose_frameworks.indication: Palliation of symptomatic distant metastases (e.g., bone pain, neurologic symptoms) [26, UM-B 3 of 4].

approaches.name: Plaque Brachytherapy
approaches.dose_fractionation: 85 Gy to apex (I-125) or 60–100 Gy (other isotopes) at low dose-rate
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: None routinely; transpupillary thermotherapy may be added adjunctively [262,285,349-351].
approaches.indication: Primary treatment for small to medium uveal melanoma (largest basal diameter ≤19 mm, thickness ≤10 mm); also for local recurrence [UM-B 1 of 4].
approaches.key_trial: COMS randomized trial: no difference in survival vs. enucleation for medium tumors [227]; 5- and 10-year disease-specific death: 10% and 18% for brachytherapy vs. 11% and 17% for enucleation [227].
approaches.toxicities: Radiation retinopathy, optic neuropathy, neovascular glaucoma, cataract, retinal detachment, vitreous hemorrhage, papillopathy, loss of visual acuity; 5-year cumulative enucleation rate ~12% [259,260,262,266,270].

approaches.name: Particle Beam Therapy (Proton, Carbon Ion, Helium Ion)
approaches.dose_fractionation: Protons: 50–70 CGyE in 4–5 fractions; Carbon ions: 60–85 CGyE in 5 fractions
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: None routinely; may combine with transpupillary thermotherapy [350].
approaches.indication: Primary treatment for tumors of all sizes, especially large or unfavorably located; also for margin-positive enucleation, intraocular/orbital recurrence; salvage for recurrence after previous RT [UM-B 2 of 4].
approaches.key_trial: UCSF-LBNL randomized trial: helium ion vs. I-125 plaque for choroidal/ciliary body melanoma; local control 100% vs. 84% at 5 years, 98% vs. 79% at 12 years [33]; another trial showed no difference in cause-specific survival between plaque and particle beam for medium tumors [8].
approaches.toxicities: Similar to brachytherapy: vitreous hemorrhage, subretinal exudation, cataract, radiation keratopathy, neovascular glaucoma, retinal detachment, radiation maculopathy, papillopathy [305,307,308,311].

approaches.name: Stereotactic Radiosurgery (Fractionated or Single-Fraction Photon)
approaches.dose_fractionation: Fractionated: 45–70 Gy in 2–5 fractions; Single-fraction: 18–45 Gy
approaches.indication: Primary treatment for uveal melanoma, especially large tumors; also for local recurrence [UM-B 2 of 4].
approaches.key_trial: Few prospective studies; retrospective comparisons suggest similar efficacy to brachytherapy and particle beam but potentially higher rates of neovascular glaucoma and radiation retinopathy [280,312,325].
approaches.toxicities: Cataract, neovascular glaucoma, radiation retinopathy, papillopathy, vitreous hemorrhage, visual acuity decline [280,312,319,324-327].

approaches.name: Metastasis-Directed Ablative RT (SABR/SBRT)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 16–24 Gy/1 fraction or 24–60 Gy/2–8 fractions using SBRT techniques with image guidance
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: May be combined with immunotherapy (e.g., IL-2) in clinical trials [516].
approaches.indication: Ablative treatment for limited (oligo-) metastases from uveal melanoma; also selective internal RT (Y-90) for liver metastases [UM-B 3 of 4].
approaches.key_trial: SABR-COMET: phase II randomized trial in oligometastatic cancers (various primaries); 5-year OS 42.3% with SABR vs. 17.7% without [515,517]. Phase II trial of SBRT+IL-2 in melanoma: ORR 54% vs. 35% with IL-2 alone [516].
approaches.toxicities: Generally well-tolerated; potential for fatigue, local pain, rare severe toxicity; Y-90 radioembolization: mild constitutional symptoms, transient LFT elevation [493,495].

approaches.name: Palliative RT for Symptomatic Metastases
approaches.dose_fractionation: 8–30 Gy in 1–10 fractions using 3D or IMRT techniques
approaches.indication: Palliation of symptomatic distant metastases (bone pain, neurologic symptoms, impending complications) [UM-B 3 of 4].
approaches.key_trial: Nonrandomized study: 90 patients with metastatic melanoma or renal cell carcinoma; pain relief 65%, neurologic symptom improvement 55% with short-course RT [26].
approaches.toxicities: Minimal; fatigue, local skin reactions, nausea.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy for metastatic uveal melanoma has limited efficacy compared to cutaneous melanoma, but certain options provide benefit. Immune checkpoint inhibitors have inferior activity in uveal melanoma, with response rates of 0%–5% for anti-PD-1 or ipilimumab monotherapy, and 5%–20% for nivolumab 1 mg/kg + ipilimumab 3 mg/kg [UMSYS-1 footnote d]. The bispecific protein tebentafusp-tebn has demonstrated an overall survival benefit in HLA-A*02:01-positive patients. No prospective studies have shown differences in overall survival between immune checkpoint inhibitor regimens. Systemic therapy options are categorized as preferred (clinical trials, tebentafusp-tebn for HLA-A*02:01-positive [category 1], ipilimumab + nivolumab), other recommended (ipilimumab, anti-PD-1 monotherapy), and useful in certain circumstances (cytotoxic regimens: dacarbazine, temozolomide, paclitaxel, albumin-bound paclitaxel, carboplatin/paclitaxel; targeted therapy: trametinib) [UMSYS-1]. The order listed does not reflect order of preference.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-line therapy for HLA-A*02:01-positive metastatic uveal melanoma
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Phase III randomized trial showed longer overall survival vs. investigator's choice (pembrolizumab, ipilimumab, or dacarbazine): 1-year OS 73% vs. 59%, median OS 21.6 vs. 16.9 months [526,537].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Ipilimumab + nivolumab (if symptomatic liver metastases may respond better [UMSYS-1 footnote c]); or agents effective for cutaneous melanoma (anti-PD-1, ipilimumab) for HLA-A*02:01-negative or after progression on tebentafusp [UMSYS-1 footnote c].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-line therapy for HLA-A*02:01-negative metastatic uveal melanoma, or after progression on tebentafusp
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Phase II trials: ORR 18% (Pelster) [534], 12-month OS 51.9% (GEM-1402) [535]; median OS 19.1 months and 12.7 months, respectively.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Ipilimumab monotherapy; anti-PD-1 monotherapy (pembrolizumab, nivolumab); or cytotoxic regimens/trametinib as useful in certain circumstances [UMSYS-1].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: After progression on immune checkpoint inhibitors (or if not candidate)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Limited efficacy of subsequent lines; no defined standard.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cytotoxic regimens: dacarbazine, temozolomide, paclitaxel, albumin-bound paclitaxel, carboplatin/paclitaxel; or trametinib [UMSYS-1].

key_regimens.name: Tebentafusp-tebn
key_regimens.setting: First-line for HLA-A*02:01-positive metastatic uveal melanoma
key_regimens.cycle_days: Cycle length not specified in guideline; clinical trials used weekly dosing [526].
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Cytokine release syndrome (especially first few cycles), rash, pruritus, hypotension, pyrexia. Hospitalization recommended for monitoring first doses [536, UMSYS-1 footnote c].

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab + Ipilimumab
key_regimens.setting: First-line or subsequent therapy for metastatic uveal melanoma
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 3 weeks for 4 cycles, then nivolumab monotherapy 3 mg/kg every 2 weeks (or 480 mg every 4 weeks) [534,535].
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, dermatitis, endocrinopathies); 40% grade 3–4 TRAEs [534]. See NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities [UMSYS-1 footnote e].

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab Monotherapy
key_regimens.setting: Option for metastatic uveal melanoma (after progression on tebentafusp or for HLA-A*02:01-negative)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Standard dosing per cutaneous melanoma (e.g., 200 mg every 3 weeks or 400 mg every 6 weeks) but not explicitly given for uveal melanoma.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events similar to other anti-PD-1 agents.

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab Monotherapy

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.route: IV or SC (nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted)

key_regimens.cycle_days: Standard dosing per cutaneous melanoma (e.g., 240 mg every 2 weeks or 480 mg every 4 weeks) but not explicitly given for uveal melanoma.

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab Monotherapy
key_regimens.cycle_days: Induction: 3 mg/kg every 3 weeks for 4 doses (or 10 mg/kg in some studies) [527,528].
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events; median OS 6.8–9.8 months in phase II trials [527,528].

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin + Paclitaxel
key_regimens.setting: Useful in certain circumstances for metastatic uveal melanoma

drugs.name: Carboplatin
drugs.dose: AUC 5–6 (from general melanoma protocols, not specified in uveal melanoma section)

key_regimens.name: Trametinib
key_regimens.setting: Useful in certain circumstances for metastatic uveal melanoma
key_regimens.cycle_days: Continuous daily dosing (typical dose 2 mg daily) [555,563].
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Rash, diarrhea, fatigue, edema, rare cardiomyopathy; limited efficacy data: ORR 0%–5.6% in small studies [555,563].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Assessment of Treatment Response and Surveillance for Recurrence
treatment_response_assessment.timing: After primary treatment of localized uveal melanoma, regular follow-up for the affected eye is recommended with color fundus photography and ultrasonography every 3–6 months for 3–5 years, then every 6–12 months if stable [UM-4 footnote x]. For distant metastasis surveillance, imaging frequency depends on risk stratification: low risk – consider every 12 months for 5 years; medium risk – every 6–12 months for 10 years; high risk – every 3–6 months for 5 years, then every 6–12 months for years 6–10 [UM-4]. After treatment for metastatic disease, imaging to assess response or progression should be performed using the same modalities recommended for baseline staging [UM-6].
response_logic: Complete response (CR) / no evidence of disease (NED): Consider observation or enrollment in clinical trials testing adjuvant therapies. Follow-up after NED follows the risk-based surveillance schedule based on primary tumor characteristics or metastatic disease status [UM-6].
Residual or progressive disease: Try other options for treatment of distant metastatic disease, including clinical trials, alternative liver-directed therapies, or systemic therapy [UM-6].
For intraocular recurrence, detection during follow-up prompts treatment as per ocular recurrence pathway (RT, enucleation, laser ablation) [UM-5].
imaging_recommendations: Liver is the most frequent site of metastasis; other sites include lungs, skin/soft tissue, and bones [UM-2 footnote i, UM-4 footnote ff].

For surveillance of distant metastasis, contrast-enhanced MRI of the abdomen (preferred), CT abdomen ± pelvis, or abdominal ultrasound may be used; modality preference determined by institutional expertise [UM-4 footnote ff].
Chest imaging can be done with CT chest without contrast or chest x-ray [UM-4 footnote ff].

For baseline staging of distant metastasis (at recurrence or initial metastatic workup), modalities of choice are MRI abdomen with IV gadolinium-based contrast (preferred), CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV iodinated contrast, CT chest with or without contrast, or whole-body FDG-PET/CT [UM-5 footnote ii]. In patients with renal failure or contrast allergy, MRI without contrast is superior to non-enhanced CT [UM-5 footnote ii].
Brain MRI with IV contrast is only performed if neurologic symptoms are present; routine CNS imaging is not recommended [UM-5 footnote ii].
Abdominal imaging should be performed with IV contrast unless contraindicated [UM-5 footnote ii].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: If extraocular recurrence or metastasis suspected, confirm histologically when possible via core needle biopsy or FNA. Consider genetic testing if it might affect treatment options [UM-5 footnote gg, UM-6 footnote gg].
For intraocular recurrence, often diagnosed clinically; additional prognostic FNA biopsy may be valuable [UM-5 footnote hh].
For progressive disease after initial treatment of metastatic disease, consider clinical trial, alternative liver-directed therapy, or systemic therapy switch [UM-6].
Consider salvage local treatment (surgery, RT, ablation) for limited progressive disease if still suitable.

prognosis.overall: Uveal melanoma is the most common primary intraocular malignancy in adults, with an incidence of approximately 5 per million annually in the United States [MS-2]. Most patients present with localized disease; fewer than 3% have detectable metastases at initial diagnosis [MS-2]. Overall 5-year disease-specific survival (DSS) ranges from 70% to 81% in large population-based analyses. For patients with early-stage disease (stage I–II), 5-year DSS is ≥85%. For those who develop distant metastatic disease, 5-year survival is estimated at <20% and has not significantly improved over recent decades [MS-2]. The risk of metastasis within 20 years after primary treatment is highly variable, ranging from approximately 20% to 70% depending on tumor stage and genetic characteristics at diagnosis [MS-2].

by_stage.context: 5-year risk of metastasis 3% to 5% [MS-2]. Corresponds to T1a N0 M0 per AJCC 8th edition [ST-2].
Includes T1b-d, T2a (Stage IIA); T2b, T3a (Stage IIB) [ST-2]. Risk of metastasis increases with T stage.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated as DSS, but 5-year risk of metastasis ≥44% [MS-2]
by_stage.context: Includes T2c-d, T3b-c, T4a (Stage IIIA); T3d, T4b-c (Stage IIIB); T4d-e (Stage IIIC) [ST-2]. High risk of distant spread.
Includes any T with N1 or any M1 [ST-2]. Median overall survival after detection of distant metastasis varies widely (3–30 months) across studies [MS-23].

prognostic_factors: AJCC T stage (tumor size, ciliary body involvement, extraocular extension) [MS-19]
Gene expression profile (GEP) class: Class 2 associated with 5- to 20-fold higher risk of metastasis than Class 1 [MS-19]
PRAME expression: risk modifier to 15-GEP signal; Class 1 PRAME(+) confers medium risk, Class 1 PRAME(-) low risk; Class 2 with PRAME may have shorter time to metastasis [UM-4]
Chromosomal abnormalities: monosomy 3 (high risk), gain of 8q (high risk, especially multiple copies), disomy 3 (low risk), gain of 6p (low risk) [MS-19]
Mutations: EIF1AX (low risk), SF3B1 (medium risk, associated with late metastases), somatic BAP1 (high risk) [MS-19]
Primary tumor largest basal diameter and thickness [MS-19]
Elevated LDH and alkaline phosphatase at time of metastasis diagnosis (associated with shorter OS) [MS-23]
prognosis.prognostic_context: Risk stratification for distant metastasis is used to guide surveillance imaging frequency. The highest risk factor present determines the follow-up pathway. Patients with low-risk features (Class 1A, disomy 3, gain 6p, EIF1AX mutation, T1) may have surveillance imaging every 12 months for 5 years. Medium-risk (Class 1B or Class 1 PRAME+, SF3B1 mutation, T2–T3) every 6–12 months for 10 years. High-risk (Class 2, monosomy 3, gain 8q, BAP1 somatic mutation, T4) every 3–6 months for 5 years, then 6–12 months for years 6–10 [UM-4]. If biopsy is not performed, medium- or high-risk pathways are followed depending on presence of clinical high-risk features [UM-4]. The 5-year risk of metastasis from population studies is 3–5% for Stage I and ≥44% for Stage III [MS-2].

disease_related.complication: Metastatic disease (most commonly to liver, then lung, bone, skin/soft tissue, lymph nodes)
disease_related.management: Liver-directed therapies (percutaneous hepatic perfusion, chemoembolization, radioembolization, immunoembolization, ablation, resection) and/or systemic therapy (tebentafusp for HLA-A*02:01-positive, immune checkpoint inhibitors, cytotoxic agents, trametinib) [UM-6]
For intraocular: RT (plaque brachytherapy or particle beam), enucleation, or laser ablation. For extraocular: surgical resection ± RT, orbital exenteration [UM-5]
Biopsy extraocular tissue if possible; options include observation, local excision, RT to orbit, or orbital exenteration [UM-3]

chemotherapy: Dacarbazine, temozolomide, paclitaxel, albumin-bound paclitaxel, carboplatin/paclitaxel – response rates <10% in metastatic uveal melanoma; median PFS <4 months [MS-31]
Cytokine release syndrome (with tebentafusp) – requires hospitalization for monitoring during initial cycles [MS-30]
immunotherapy: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ipilimumab, nivolumab, pembrolizumab, ipilimumab+nivolumab): inferior activity vs cutaneous melanoma; response rates 0%–5% for monotherapy, 5%–20% for combination [UMSYS-1]
Grade 3–4 immune-related adverse events: 10% with ipilimumab [MS-29], 40% with nivolumab+ipilimumab [MS-30]
Tebentafusp: cytokine release syndrome common; treatment discontinuation due to adverse events in 2% of patients; no treatment-related deaths [MS-30]
targeted_therapy: Trametinib (MEK inhibitor): limited data; response rate ~5.6% in one phase II study; median PFS 3.6 months [MS-32]
Selumetinib: response rate 14% in phase II; median PFS 3.7 months [MS-32]
surgery: Enucleation: pain, hemorrhage, nausea/vomiting, cardiovascular/pulmonary problems, urinary retention, fever, local surgical issues [MS-13]
Orbital exenteration: significant morbidity, debated survival benefit [MS-17]
radiation: Brachytherapy/particle beam RT: radiation retinopathy, optic neuropathy, papillopathy, maculopathy, neovascular glaucoma, retinal detachment, cataract, vitreous hemorrhage [MS-14-15]
Loss of visual acuity: approximately 50% of patients lose ≥6 lines after 3 years with brachytherapy [MS-15]
Secondary enucleation: 5-year cumulative rate ~12% after brachytherapy, often due to pain or tumor progression [MS-14]
SRS: higher rates of neovascular glaucoma, radiation retinopathy, papillopathy vs brachytherapy [MS-16]
Management of radiation complications: intravitreal anti-VEGF (bevacizumab, ranibizumab, aflibercept), intravitreal corticosteroids, panretinal photocoagulation [MS-16]

supportive_care.overview: Supportive and palliative care should be integrated for patients with advanced or metastatic uveal melanoma. The NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care are referenced for best supportive care [UM-6]. Specific supportive care recommendations within the uveal melanoma guideline are limited; most are extrapolated from general oncology principles or the cutaneous melanoma guideline.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Not addressed in the uveal melanoma guideline. Standard nutritional support per institutional protocols for patients undergoing systemic therapy or liver-directed treatments.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Not specifically provided. For patients receiving dacarbazine, temozolomide, or carboplatin/paclitaxel, antiemetic prophylaxis should follow standard institutional guidelines.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Not addressed. Should be used per standard practice for chemotherapy regimens with neutropenic risk (e.g., carboplatin/paclitaxel).
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Not addressed. Consider per standard guidelines for hospitalized patients with cancer.
supportive_care.pain_management: Palliative radiotherapy for symptomatic metastases can be used: doses of 8–30 Gy in 1–10 fractions [UM-B 3 of 4]. Pain relief from bone metastases achieved in 65% of patients with short-course RT [MS-29].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Providers may discuss mental health resources with patients; NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship are referenced [MS-20]. No specific psychological interventions detailed.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After definitive treatment of primary uveal melanoma, follow-up includes evaluation of the treated eye and surveillance for distant metastasis. The affected eye should be imaged with color fundus photography and ultrasonography every 3–6 months for 3–5 years, then every 6–12 months thereafter if stable. Serial orbital MRI may be used as clinically indicated. Frequency depends on tumor size, location (e.g., juxtapapillary, ciliary body involvement), and risk of radiation-related complications [UM-4]. The contralateral eye is not at increased risk and should be followed with routine ophthalmologic care including eye protection (e.g., polycarbonate glasses) [UM-4]. Liver function tests (LFTs) may be considered but have poor sensitivity for early detection of liver metastases [UM-4]. For patients who achieve no evidence of disease after treatment of isolated metastases, follow-up similar to post-primary treatment is recommended, with frequency guided by original risk stratification [UM-6].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Most uveal melanomas are characterized by early micrometastasis and a variable latency period before overt metastasis emerges. Local recurrence is rare (<10%), but distant metastasis occurs in 20–70% of patients within 20 years depending on stage and genetic factors [MS-18]. The rationale for surveillance is to detect distant disease at an earlier, potentially more treatable stage, although it remains unclear whether early detection improves survival given the limited efficacy of current treatments for metastatic disease. Some retrospective studies suggest survival benefit for patients whose metastases are detected while asymptomatic, but others show no correlation [MS-21].
late_effects_screening: Radiation-related sequelae: retinopathy, optic neuropathy, neovascular glaucoma, cataract – may occur at any time after treatment; monitored by scheduled ophthalmologic exams [UM-4]
Secondary primary malignancies: patients are at increased risk for other cancers; regular cancer screening as per age and sex recommendations [MS-22]
Visual acuity loss: annual measurement and documentation
Prosthesis fit and socket health after enucleation
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Local recurrence after RT is most common within 2–4 years but can occur as late as 12 years; median time to recurrence <2 years for particle beam therapy [MS-15]. Distant metastasis most frequently involves the liver (90% of cases), then lung (20–30%), bone (16–18%), skin/soft tissue (11–12%), and lymph nodes (10–11%). Brain metastases occur in 4–5% of patients who develop distant disease [MS-19]. The disease-free interval from primary treatment to metastasis is highly variable, ranging from months to many years.

clinical_follow_up_schedule: Affected eye: color fundus photography and ultrasonography every 3–6 months for 3–5 years, then every 6–12 months if stable [UM-4]
Contralateral eye: routine ophthalmologic care (no increased risk) [UM-4]
Low risk for distant metastasis (Class 1A, Class 1 PRAME(-), disomy 3, gain 6p, EIF1AX mutation, T1): consider surveillance imaging every 12 months for 5 years, then as clinically indicated [UM-4]
Medium risk (Class 1B, Class 1 PRAME(+), SF3B1 mutation, T2–T3): consider surveillance imaging every 6–12 months for 10 years, then as clinically indicated [UM-4]

High risk (Class 2, monosomy 3, gain 8q, BAP1 somatic mutation, T4): consider surveillance imaging every 3–6 months for 5 years, then every 6–12 months for years 6–10, then as clinically indicated [UM-4]
imaging_strategy: Preferred modality for abdominal imaging: contrast-enhanced MRI of the abdomen [UM-4]
Alternative: CT abdomen ± pelvis, or abdominal ultrasound (modality preference determined by institutional expertise) [UM-4]
Chest imaging: CT chest without contrast or chest x-ray [UM-4]
All scans should be performed with IV contrast unless contraindicated
Whole-body FDG-PET/CT is an option for staging at recurrence or metastatic workup [UM-5]
Brain MRI with IV contrast only if neurologic symptoms present; routine CNS imaging not recommended [UM-5]
laboratory_monitoring: Liver function tests (LFTs) may be considered but have poor sensitivity for early detection of liver metastases (COMS: sensitivity 14.7%, specificity 92.3%) [MS-21]
Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) had the highest diagnostic attributes among LFTs [MS-21]
Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and ALP at time of metastasis diagnosis may have prognostic value [UM-6A]
supportive_follow_up: Routine eye protection for the remaining eye (polycarbonate glasses) [UM-4]
Mental health resources discussion per NCCN Survivorship guidelines [MS-20]
Consider referral for genetic counseling if familial syndrome suspected (BAP1, PALB2, POT1, MBD4) [UM-A 1 of 2]

special_situations.situation: Choroidal nevus or indeterminate small lesion (T1 equivalent) with <3 risk factors for growth
special_situations.key_message: Observation is appropriate for lesions with uncertain diagnosis, especially small tumors with fewer than 3 risk factors (symptoms, thickness >2 mm, diameter >5 mm, subretinal fluid, orange pigment, margin within 3 mm of disc, ultrasound hollowness) [UM-1].
special_situations.management: Re-evaluate every 2–6 months initially, then close follow-up for 5 years, then annually if stable. Testing includes comprehensive eye exam, color fundus photography, ocular ultrasound, autofluorescence, OCT, fluorescein angiography as needed [UM-1].
special_situations.evidence: Studies show that deferring treatment until growth or malignant features develop is associated with very low risk of metastasis and death [MS-11].

special_situations.situation: Familial uveal melanoma / germline pathogenic variants
special_situations.key_message: A small percentage of uveal melanoma patients have hereditary predisposition. Germline variants in BAP1, PALB2, POT1, and MBD4 have been identified [MS-4]. BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome also increases risk for mesothelioma, cutaneous melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, and cholangiocarcinoma [MS-4].
special_situations.management: Evaluate for hereditary syndrome if: early age (<30), personal/family history of other primary cancers, multiple family members with uveal melanoma. Refer for genetic counseling and testing [UM-A 1 of 2].
special_situations.evidence: Walpole et al. 2018 [ref 98], Abdel-Rahman et al. 2020 [ref 106], Nathan et al. 2021 [ref 107], Derrien et al. 2021 [ref 108].

special_situations.situation: Extraocular extension at time of enucleation
special_situations.key_message: Extraocular extension is present in ~3% of cases at diagnosis, more common in higher T stages, and is associated with poor prognosis [MS-17].
special_situations.management: For microscopic positive/close margins without gross residual: observe or consider RT to orbit. For visible extraocular tumor: biopsy if possible, then consider observation, local excision, RT to orbit, or orbital exenteration [UM-3].
special_situations.evidence: COMS trial data show 2% unexpected extrascleral extension despite thorough workup [MS-17]. Retrospective studies support postoperative RT for young patients and those with thicker tumors [MS-18].

special_situations.key_message: Tebentafusp-tebn is a bispecific gp100-HLA-A*02:01-directed T-cell receptor and CD3 T-cell engager that showed improved overall survival vs investigator's choice in a phase III trial [MS-30].
special_situations.management: First-line for HLA-A*02:01-positive patients (category 1). For symptomatic patients or those with liver-dominant disease, liver-directed therapy or ipilimumab/nivolumab may be considered first due to low response rates with tebentafusp [UMSYS-1].
special_situations.evidence: Nathan et al. N Engl J Med 2021;385:1196-1206 [ref 526]. Median OS 21.6 vs 16.9 months (control); 3-year OS 27% vs 18%. PFS at 6 months 31% vs 19% [MS-30].

special_situations.key_message: When the bulk of metastatic disease is confined to the liver, liver-directed therapies are recommended and may offer better outcomes than systemic therapy alone [MS-24].
special_situations.management: Clinical trial preferred. Options include: melphalan percutaneous hepatic perfusion (PHP), chemoembolization, radioembolization, immunoembolization, ablative procedures (thermal ablation, cryotherapy), resection, and/or RT [UM-6].
special_situations.evidence: Phase III trial of PHP (melphalan) showed improved response rate (36.3% vs 12.5%) and PFS (9 vs 3.1 months) vs best alternative care, but median OS not significantly different (19.3 vs 14.5 months, p=0.14) [UM-6A]. Phase II data for radioembolization: disease control 87% in first-line, median OS 18.5 months [MS-28].

clinical_pearls: Uveal melanoma is molecularly distinct from cutaneous melanoma: BRAF and NRAS mutations are rare; >80% harbor GNAQ or GNA11 mutations. Targeted therapies effective in BRAF-mutant cutaneous melanoma are not applicable [UMSYS-1].

The liver is the most frequent site of metastasis (90% of cases). Baseline and surveillance abdominal imaging should focus on the liver, with contrast-enhanced MRI preferred over CT or ultrasound [UM-4].

Risk stratification for distant metastasis using gene expression profiling (GEP) and chromosome status strongly predicts outcomes and guides surveillance intensity. PRAME expression modifies GEP risk class [UM-4].

Tebentafusp is the only systemic therapy with a proven overall survival benefit in a randomized phase III trial for metastatic uveal melanoma, but it is limited to HLA-A*02:01-positive patients (approximately 50% of Caucasians) [MS-30].
Immune checkpoint inhibitors have significantly lower activity in uveal melanoma compared to cutaneous melanoma; combination ipilimumab + nivolumab yields response rates of 5–20% [UMSYS-1].

Biopsy of the primary tumor for prognostic analysis is strongly considered before radiation, as RT may alter molecular features. Molecular/chromosomal testing is preferred over cytology alone [UM-2A].

For small (<2.5 mm thick) choroidal lesions without high-risk features, initial observation with close follow-up is safe and does not worsen prognosis if treatment is deferred until growth or malignant transformation occurs [MS-11].

Liver-directed therapies (melphalan PHP, chemoembolization, radioembolization) are important options for patients with hepatic-dominant metastases and should be considered early, especially in symptomatic patients or those with low disease burden [UM-6A].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Melanoma: Uveal, Version 2.2026
guidelines_resources.notes: Comprehensive recommendations for diagnosis, workup, primary treatment, surveillance, and management of recurrent/metastatic uveal melanoma. Updated annually.

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition (2017) – Choroidal and Ciliary Body Melanoma Staging
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides TNM definitions and prognostic stage groups for choroidal and ciliary body melanoma. Not applicable for iris melanoma [MS-2].

guidelines_resources.title: Uveal Melanoma Synoptic Report Recommendations
guidelines_resources.notes: Standardized template for pathologic evaluation of enucleation specimens. Available at https://documents.cap.org/protocols/Uvea.Mel_4.2.0.0.REL_CAPCP.pdf [UM-2A].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for best supportive/palliative care options in patients with metastatic uveal melanoma [UM-6].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for management of immune-related adverse events from checkpoint inhibitor therapy [UMSYS-1].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for mental health and psychosocial support for patients during follow-up [MS-20].

key_trials.full_name: Collaborative Ocular Melanoma Study: Iodine-125 Brachytherapy vs Enucleation for Medium Choroidal Melanoma
key_trials.population: Patients with choroidal melanoma 2.5–10.0 mm apical height (2.5–8.0 mm if peripapillary) and ≤16 mm basal diameter, no extrascleral extension ≥2 mm
key_trials.key_result: No significant difference in 12-year mortality rates between arms. DSS at 5 years: 90% (brachytherapy) vs 89% (enucleation); 10-year DSS: 82% vs 83% [ref 227].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Local recurrence rate 10.3% with brachytherapy vs 1% with enucleation; enucleation rate after brachytherapy ~12% at 5 years [MS-14].
key_trials.practice_change: Established plaque brachytherapy as a standard globe-conserving alternative to enucleation for medium-sized choroidal melanoma.

key_trials.full_name: Overall Survival Benefit with Tebentafusp in Metastatic Uveal Melanoma
key_trials.population: Previously untreated HLA-A*02:01-positive metastatic uveal melanoma patients
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 21.6 months (tebentafusp) vs 16.9 months (control); HR 0.51 (95% CI not fully reported, but significant). Estimated 1-year OS 73% vs 59%. 3-year OS 27% vs 18% [ref 526, 537].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PFS at 6 months: 31% vs 19%. Response rate 9% vs 5%. No treatment-related deaths. Cytokine release syndrome common but managed [MS-30].
key_trials.practice_change: First systemic therapy to show OS benefit in metastatic uveal melanoma; became first-line standard for HLA-A*02:01-positive patients. FDA approved.

key_trials.full_name: Isolated Hepatic Perfusion with Melphalan for Patients with Isolated Uveal Melanoma Liver Metastases (SCANDIUM Trial)
key_trials.population: Patients with isolated uveal melanoma liver metastases (<50% liver involvement)
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 9.0 months (PHP) vs 3.1 months (best alternative care); p<0.001. Response rate 36.3% vs 12.5% [UM-6A].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median OS 19.3 vs 14.5 months (p=0.14, not statistically significant). Required overnight ICU stay for hemodynamic monitoring [UM-6A].
key_trials.practice_change: Demonstrated that PHP is an effective liver-directed therapy for hepatic-dominant metastatic uveal melanoma, with improved PFS and response rate, though OS benefit not confirmed.

key_trials.full_name: Collaborative Ocular Melanoma Study: Pre-enucleation Radiation for Large Choroidal Melanoma
key_trials.population: Patients with large choroidal melanoma (height ≥2 mm and diameter ≥16 mm; or height ≥10 mm any diameter; or height ≥8 mm <2 mm from optic disc)
key_trials.intervention: Pre-enucleation radiation (20 Gy in 5 fractions) followed by enucleation
key_trials.key_result: No significant survival benefit from pre-enucleation RT. 5-year DSS: 72% both arms; 10-year DSS: 60% vs 61% [ref 245, 246].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Local recurrence rate <1% after enucleation regardless of RT. Pre-enucleation RT did not reduce orbital recurrence or improve outcomes [MS-13].
key_trials.practice_change: Pre-enucleation RT was not adopted as standard practice. Enucleation alone remains adequate for large tumors without extraocular extension.

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab plus Ipilimumab for Treatment-Naive Metastatic Uveal Melanoma (Spanish Multidisciplinary Melanoma Group)
key_trials.population: Treatment-naïve metastatic uveal melanoma, ECOG PS 0/1
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab 1 mg/kg + ipilimumab 3 mg/kg every 3 weeks x 4, then nivolumab 3 mg/kg every 2 weeks
key_trials.key_result: 12-month OS rate 51.9%. Median OS 12.7 months, median PFS 3.0 months. Stable disease in 51.9% of patients [ref 535].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR not specified as primary, but best response included partial responses in some. PFS influenced by higher LDH. Grade 3-4 treatment-related adverse events in 40%.
key_trials.practice_change: Confirmed that combination immunotherapy has modest activity in first-line metastatic uveal melanoma, with OS comparable to historical chemotherapy benchmarks but better than single-agent PD-1.

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab and Ipilimumab in Metastatic Uveal Melanoma (MD Anderson)
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab 1 mg/kg + ipilimumab 3 mg/kg x 4, then nivolumab maintenance
key_trials.key_result: ORR 18% (1 CR, 5 PR). Median PFS 5.5 months, median OS 19.1 months. Grade 3-4 treatment-related adverse events in 40% [ref 534].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Responses observed even in patients with liver metastases. Durable responses in some patients.
key_trials.practice_change: Provided evidence that combination checkpoint blockade can produce meaningful responses in a subset of patients with metastatic uveal melanoma, even after prior therapy.

key_trials.full_name: Selumetinib in Combination with Dacarbazine in Patients with Metastatic Uveal Melanoma: A Phase III Multicenter Randomized Trial
key_trials.key_result: No significant improvement in PFS or OS with combination. Median PFS not improved vs dacarbazine alone [ref 522].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Response rates low in both arms. Treatment was well tolerated.
key_trials.practice_change: Failed to confirm earlier phase II signal. Indicated that MEK inhibition may not be broadly effective in uveal melanoma, though selumetinib single-agent showed modest activity in prior phase II.
